Samson's story in this chapter is a descent from strength into utter degradation—and it tells us something piercing about the difference between God's gifting and God's presence. Samson enters Gaza with the confidence of a man accustomed to miraculous power, and he leaves it a blind prisoner grinding grain for his enemies. The irony cuts deep: the man who could snap iron shackles and tear down city gates falls completely to a woman who simply asks for the truth. Delilah is relentless, pressing him daily until he is "sick to death," and Samson—mighty Samson—surrenders not because his strength fails but because his will does. He chooses comfort over the Nazirite vow that defined his entire calling. He loves this woman more than he fears God. And so he tells her everything. The moment he speaks the secret, he signs his own destruction. This is not a story of strength overcome by cunning alone. It is a story of a man who abandoned God's purpose long before Delilah shaved his head.

What makes this chapter unbearable is verse 20: "But he did not know that the LORD had departed from him." Samson wakes from sleep expecting the same miraculous deliverance that has rescued him countless times before. He still has his strength—or thinks he does—until he actually tries to move and realizes the terrible truth. The Lord has left. This is not about hair or supernatural power failing mechanically. This is about God withdrawing His presence from a man who stopped living for Him. Samson had become a prisoner to his own appetites long before the Philistines locked him in chains. He had judged Israel, yes, but he never truly submitted to the God who raised him up. His strength was always meant to serve a purpose beyond himself, and he squandered it on personal vengeance and forbidden desire.

Yet God is not finished with Samson, and that haunts this chapter in the most unexpected way. After the Philistines humble him, blind him, and enslave him, his hair grows back. Verse 22 is so brief it could be missed: "However, the hair of his head began to grow back after it had been shaved." This is not automatic restoration. This is a God who gives His wayward servant one last chance. When Samson calls out from blindness and captivity—"O Lord GOD, please remember me"—he is finally, truly asking God for something. Not for comfort or victory, but for one more opportunity to serve God's purpose, even if it means his own death. The strength returns, the pillars fall, and Samson accomplishes in his death what his life never fully managed: he becomes a weapon entirely in God's hands, with no personal agenda left. His final act judges Israel not by his individual dominance but by a single, desperate, surrendered prayer. This is what it looks like when a broken man finally lets God be God.
